requirements: The core contributions of the paper 'LLM-42: Enabling Determinism in LLM Inference with Verified Speculation' have been reproduced.

sub_tasks.requirements: The environment and infrastructure required for the experiments have been set up.
Code has been implemented to run on a system equipped with NVIDIA H100 PCIe GPUs with 80 GB HBM3 memory.

verification.spec: Run `nvidia-smi --query-gpu=name,memory.total --format=csv` and verify at least one NVIDIA H100 PCIe GPU with 80GB HBM3 memory is present. Also verify CUDA can initialize a tensor on the GPU via `torch.cuda.is_available()` and `torch.cuda.get_device_name(0)` containing 'H100'.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code is configured to use up to four H100 PCIe GPUs with tensor parallelism for multi-GPU experiments.

verification.spec: Inspect the LLM-42 launch configuration / server startup args and verify the `--tp-size` (or equivalent tensor-parallel) flag is plumbed through and accepts values up to 4. Run `nvidia-smi -L` to confirm 4 H100 GPUs visible, then start the server with tp=4 and confirm it loads the model without error.

sub_tasks.requirements: SGLang serving framework version 0.5.3rc0 is installed and available as the base system to build LLM-42 on top of.

verification.spec: Run `python -c 'import sglang; print(sglang.__version__)'` and verify the version string is '0.5.3rc0'. Also confirm `pip show sglang` reports the same version.

sub_tasks.requirements: FlashAttention-3 attention kernel is available and used in all experiments.

verification.spec: Run `python -c 'import flash_attn_interface; print(flash_attn_interface.__version__)'` (or equivalent FA3 import path), verify it imports without error, and grep the LLM-42 server config to confirm attention backend is set to 'fa3' / FlashAttention-3.

sub_tasks.requirements: Required models and datasets have been obtained.
The meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct model has been obtained and can be loaded for inference (32 query heads, 8 KV heads, 32 layers).

verification.spec: Load the model via `transformers.AutoConfig.from_pretrained('meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct')` and assert `num_attention_heads==32`, `num_key_value_heads==8`, `num_hidden_layers==32`. Then load the model weights with SGLang and confirm it initializes successfully.

sub_tasks.requirements: The ShareGPT (anon8231489123/ShareGPT_Vicuna_unfiltered) dataset has been obtained.

verification.spec: Verify the ShareGPT dataset (anon8231489123/ShareGPT_Vicuna_unfiltered) is present locally. Check file exists, JSON parses, and contains conversation entries. Verify total number of usable requests matches ~92812 reported.

sub_tasks.requirements: The ArXiv long-document summarization dataset has been obtained (test split with ~5941 requests).

verification.spec: Verify the ArXiv summarization dataset test split is downloaded. Check that the file exists, can be loaded, and contains ~5941 requests. Confirm tokenized input lengths align with the reported mean of ~7017 and median ~6435.

sub_tasks.requirements: The meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct tokenizer is used for all experiments.

verification.spec: Load tokenizer using AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained('meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct') and verify it instantiates successfully and is used consistently across benchmark scripts (grep for the model name in dataset preparation code).

sub_tasks.requirements: Synthetic workload generators are implemented for fixed input/output length configurations: (in=512, out=256), (in=1024, out=256), (in=1024, out=512), (in=2048, out=256), (in=2048, out=512), (in=4096, out=512).

verification.spec: Verify synthetic workload generator script exists and produces request sets for each of six fixed (input_length, output_length) configurations: (512,256), (1024,256), (1024,512), (2048,256), (2048,512), (4096,512). Run the generator for each config and check token counts match exactly using the Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct tokenizer.

sub_tasks.requirements: The motivation experiments measuring the cost of batch-invariant computation have been reproduced.

A microbenchmark has been implemented to compare cuBLAS-based GEMM (via torch.mm) versus the Triton batch-invariant GEMM kernel for the down-projection of Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct's FFN at varying token counts.

verification.spec: Run microbenchmark script that allocates input/weight tensors matching Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct down-projection (input dim 14336, output dim 4096) for varying token counts (powers of 2 up to 8192). Time both torch.mm (cuBLAS) and the Triton batch-invariant GEMM from Thinking Machines. Verify the script produces TFLOPS numbers for both kernels at each token count.

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced GEMM benchmark shows cuBLAS reaches up to ~527 TFLOPS while the Triton batch-invariant kernel peaks at ~194 TFLOPS (a slowdown of ~63%).

verification.spec: Benchmark cuBLAS GEMM via `torch.mm` and the Triton batch-invariant GEMM (from He et al.) on matrix shapes corresponding to Llama-3.1-8B FFN down-projection (e.g., M up to 4096, K=14336, N=4096). Measure TFLOPS at peak token count. Verify cuBLAS reaches ~527 TFLOPS peak.

sub_tasks.requirements: An RMSNorm microbenchmark has been implemented comparing SGLang Python-based batch-invariant RMSNorm, Thinking Machines' Triton-based batch-invariant kernel, and SGLang's default CUDA RMSNorm kernel for token counts in {1, 8, 32, 128, 256, 512, 1024, 2048, 4096}.

verification.spec: Run RMSNorm microbenchmark that measures execution time at token counts {1,8,32,128,256,512,1024,2048,4096} for three implementations: (1) SGLang Python batch-invariant RMSNorm, (2) Thinking Machines Triton batch-invariant kernel, (3) SGLang default fused CUDA RMSNorm. Verify all three produce latency measurements for each token count.

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced RMSNorm benchmark shows the Python batch-invariant implementation is up to ~7x slower than the non-batch-invariant CUDA kernel.

verification.spec: Benchmark RMSNorm latency for token counts {1,8,32,128,256,512,1024,2048,4096} using (a) SGLang's Python batch-invariant impl and (b) SGLang's default fused CUDA kernel. Compute max ratio Python/CUDA across token counts; verify it reaches approximately 7x.

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced RMSNorm benchmark shows the Triton batch-invariant kernel remains up to ~50% slower than the fused CUDA implementation.

verification.spec: Benchmark RMSNorm latency for token counts {1..4096} comparing the Triton batch-invariant kernel (He et al.) vs SGLang's fused CUDA kernel. Compute max relative slowdown (triton_time - cuda_time)/cuda_time; verify it is up to ~0.5 (50%).

sub_tasks.requirements: End-to-end decode throughput benchmark (Figure 5) is implemented with three scenarios: (1) 10 concurrent non-deterministic requests, (2) 11 concurrent non-deterministic requests, (3) 11 requests where only 1 requires determinism.

verification.spec: Run end-to-end decode throughput benchmark with Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct serving via SGLang/LLM-42. Issue (1) 10 concurrent non-deterministic requests, (2) 11 concurrent non-deterministic requests, (3) 11 requests with 1 marked is_deterministic=True. For each scenario, measure decode throughput in tokens/sec. Verify the script produces three throughput numbers matching the three scenarios described in Figure 5.

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced throughput benchmark confirms ~845 tokens/s for 10 non-deterministic requests.

verification.spec: Start SGLang non-deterministic server with Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct on a single H100. Issue 10 concurrent long-decoding requests, measure aggregate output tokens/sec at steady state during the decode phase. Verify ~845 tokens/s.

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced throughput benchmark confirms ~931 tokens/s for 11 non-deterministic requests (~10% improvement).

verification.spec: Start SGLang non-deterministic server with Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct on a single H100. Issue 11 concurrent long-decoding requests, measure aggregate output tokens/sec at steady state during the decode phase. Verify ~931 tokens/s (about 10% higher than the 10-request case).

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced throughput benchmark confirms SGLang deterministic mode collapses to ~415 tokens/s (a ~56% degradation) when one request among 11 requires determinism.

verification.spec: Start SGLang in deterministic mode (batch-invariant kernels enabled) with Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct on a single H100. Issue 11 concurrent decode requests where at least one requires deterministic output. Measure aggregate decode throughput; verify ~415 tokens/s (about 56% drop vs non-deterministic 11-request baseline of 931).

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced throughput benchmark shows LLM-42 achieves ~911 tokens/s (~2.2x higher than SGLang deterministic and within ~3% of non-deterministic best case) for the 1-of-11 deterministic scenario.

verification.spec: Start LLM-42 with Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct on H100. Submit 11 concurrent decode requests with is_deterministic=True for exactly 1 of them. Measure aggregate decode tokens/sec; verify ~911 tokens/s (within 3% of non-deterministic 931 baseline and ~2.2x SGLang-Deterministic 415).

sub_tasks.requirements: The observational analysis of non-determinism in LLM inference has been reproduced.

An experiment is implemented that runs 350 requests at batch size 1 to obtain ground-truth output tokens, then re-runs the same requests under dynamic batching at 6 QPS using Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct on ShareGPT with fixed output length of 512 tokens.

verification.spec: Run experiment script that (a) executes 350 ShareGPT requests with batch_size=1 and fixed output length 512 using Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct to obtain ground-truth output token sequences, then (b) re-runs the same 350 requests through dynamic batching at 6 QPS with output length 512. Confirm both runs produce token sequences of length 512 per request and are saved for comparison.

sub_tasks.requirements: Metrics for 'first consistent span' and 'second consistent span' between dynamic-batch runs and ground-truth reference are computed per-request.

verification.spec: Import the analysis function that computes first_consistent_span and second_consistent_span. Construct synthetic reference and run-token sequences where divergence positions are known (e.g., ref=[1,2,3,4,5], run=[1,2,9,4,5] -> first_span=2, second_span=2). Verify the function returns expected span lengths and handles all-match (full length) and immediate-mismatch (zero) cases.

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced results show many requests have hundreds of initial tokens matching the reference (first consistent span), with some requests matching all 512 tokens.

verification.spec: Reproduce the experiment of running 350 ShareGPT requests with batch_size=1 reference vs 6 QPS dynamic batching using Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct. Compute first_consistent_span per request. Verify that the median first_consistent_span across the 350 requests is at least 100 tokens (i.e., 'hundreds') and that at least a few requests match the full 512 tokens.

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced results show the second consistent span is near zero for most requests, confirming rapid divergence after the first inconsistent token.

verification.spec: From the 350-request reference vs. dynamic-batching experiment, compute the second_consistent_span across all requests. Verify that the median second_consistent_span is <= 5 tokens (near zero) confirming rapid divergence after the first inconsistency.

sub_tasks.requirements: The LLM-42 method (decode-verify-rollback protocol) is implemented correctly.
A per-request API flag `is_deterministic` (True|False, default False) is exposed allowing users to request determinism on a per-request basis.

verification.spec: Inspect the LLM-42 request API schema (e.g., HTTP endpoint payload definition or Python client). Verify a field named `is_deterministic` of type bool is present with default False. Submit two requests: one with is_deterministic=True and one with the field omitted; verify the server accepts both and routes appropriately (e.g., second goes through fast path without verification).

sub_tasks.requirements: The prefill phase for deterministic requests is made deterministic by avoiding arbitrary inter-request batching during prefill, so prefill produces consistent first tokens.

verification.spec: Submit two prompts of equal length with is_deterministic=True, run them in two separate server invocations (or back-to-back with different batch composition during prefill). Compare the first generated token (T0) across runs — it must be bitwise identical. Also inspect the scheduler code to confirm deterministic requests' prefill is not co-batched arbitrarily with other prefill tokens.

sub_tasks.requirements: Decode phase uses standard fast-path autoregressive decoding with dynamic batching (non-deterministic, high-throughput) for deterministic requests' candidate tokens.

verification.spec: Inspect the LLM-42 scheduler's decode path. Send a single request with is_deterministic=True alongside several non-deterministic requests. Confirm via logs/instrumentation that the deterministic request's candidate decode tokens are produced through the same fast-path forward pass as non-deterministic requests, batched together with them (no separate batch-invariant kernel calls during decode).

sub_tasks.requirements: Verification is skipped entirely for requests that have is_deterministic=False; only fast-path decode is used for them.

verification.spec: Submit a mix of requests with is_deterministic=True and is_deterministic=False. Instrument the verifier to record which request IDs enter verification. Verify that non-deterministic requests never appear in verification batches and that their output is returned via fast-path decode only.

sub_tasks.requirements: Operator-level determinism mechanisms required by LLM-42 are configured correctly.
FlashAttention-3 attention kernel is configured with num_splits=1 during the verification pass to ensure consistent reductions, while default settings are used during fast-path decode.

verification.spec: Inspect the LLM-42 attention call sites: confirm that the verification forward pass invokes FlashAttention-3 with num_splits=1 (e.g., via config flag or kernel argument), while the fast-path decode invocation uses default num_splits. Verify by searching the source and/or instrumenting kernel calls to log the num_splits parameter during a verification step vs. a decode step.

sub_tasks.requirements: A deterministic AllReduce implementation is used (multimem-based AllReduce where supported, or tree-based AllReduce with fixed NCCL configuration such as num_channels=1 and fixed protocol on older platforms).

verification.spec: Inspect the communication layer setup: verify that the AllReduce path selects multimem-based AllReduce when CUDA>=13.0 / NVLS is available, and otherwise falls back to a tree-based AllReduce configured with num_channels=1 and a fixed NCCL protocol. Check by reading initialization code and inspecting NCCL environment variables / collective config used at runtime.

sub_tasks.requirements: A deterministic sampler is implemented: at temperature=0 it returns argmax with tie-break to the first maximal index; for non-greedy sampling, the SGLang multinomial_with_seed function (Gumbel-noise perturbation with a seeded hash) is used instead of torch.multinomial.

verification.spec: Unit-test the sampler: (1) with temperature=0 and a logit vector containing duplicate maxima, confirm the returned index is the first maximal index (argmax tie-break to lowest index); (2) with temperature>0, call multinomial_with_seed twice with identical (logits, seed) and confirm identical sample indices; with different seeds, confirm sample variability. Also confirm torch.multinomial is not invoked in the deterministic path.

sub_tasks.requirements: Existing optimized GEMM, RMSNorm, and FusedMoE kernels are reused unchanged from SGLang in the fast-path (no batch-invariant rewrites required).

verification.spec: Diff the LLM-42 source against upstream SGLang v0.5.3rc0 for files implementing GEMM, RMSNorm, and FusedMoE kernels. Confirm those kernel files are unchanged (or only reused via import) and no batch-invariant replacement kernels are added for these ops. Optionally instrument the fast-path forward to log kernel names used and confirm they match SGLang default kernel names.

sub_tasks.requirements: Baseline systems (SGLang-Deterministic and SGLang-Non-Deterministic) are set up correctly for comparison.
SGLang-Non-Deterministic baseline is set up using SGLang v0.5.3rc0 with standard (non-batch-invariant) kernels.

verification.spec: Install SGLang v0.5.3rc0, launch a server with meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct on an H100 with deterministic mode OFF (default), and send a sample request. Confirm server starts, the model loads, a completion is returned, and that the configuration flag for batch-invariant/deterministic mode is disabled (e.g., via /get_server_info or startup logs).

sub_tasks.requirements: SGLang-Deterministic baseline is set up using SGLang v0.5.3rc0 with deterministic mode enabled (batch-invariant kernels).

verification.spec: Install SGLang v0.5.3rc0 and launch a server with meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct on an H100 with deterministic mode enabled (batch-invariant kernels). Send the same prompt twice under different concurrent loads and confirm bit-identical outputs; confirm logs/config show batch-invariant kernels active.

sub_tasks.requirements: Evaluation metrics for offline and online inference are implemented.
Offline throughput (tokens/sec) measurement is implemented for executing a fixed number of requests to completion.

verification.spec: Run the offline benchmark script with a fixed number of requests (e.g., 4096) and confirm it (1) submits all requests to the server, (2) measures wall-clock time from first submission to last completion, (3) sums input+output tokens, and (4) reports throughput in tokens/sec. Verify the reported number is positive and matches an independent computation from raw timing logs.

sub_tasks.requirements: End-to-end request latency measurement is implemented for online inference and reported as CDF.

verification.spec: Run the online benchmark with a Poisson/QPS load generator, collect per-request (submit_time, completion_time) tuples, compute e2e latency = completion-submit per request, and confirm a CDF is generated (sorted latency vs. cumulative probability) matching expected format of Figure 11. Verify CDF endpoints (min/max) match raw latency data.

sub_tasks.requirements: Time-to-first-token (TTFT) measurement is implemented and reported at P50/P75/P90 percentiles.

verification.spec: Run the online benchmark and confirm the harness records per-request TTFT (time from request submission to first streamed token) and reports P50/P75/P90 percentiles. Validate by manually computing these percentiles from raw TTFT data and matching the reported values.

sub_tasks.requirements: Rollback count per request and recomputed-tokens count per request are tracked and reported by LLM-42.

verification.spec: Run LLM-42 with deterministic traffic on a small workload (e.g., 100 requests) and confirm the server logs/metrics emit per-request rollback count and per-request recomputed-token count. Verify these counters are integers >= 0 and sum to global totals that match aggregate reported values.

sub_tasks.requirements: Offline inference experiments (Section 5.1) have been executed and reproduced.
Each offline experiment runs 4096 requests to completion across the 8 workload configurations (ArXiv, ShareGPT, and 6 synthetic input/output length combinations).

verification.spec: Execute the offline benchmark script for each of 8 workload configs (ArXiv test split, ShareGPT, in=512/out=256, in=1024/out=256, in=1024/out=512, in=2048/out=256, in=2048/out=512, in=4096/out=512) with N=4096 requests each. Confirm all 4096 requests complete (no truncation or drops) in every run.

sub_tasks.requirements: Deterministic-traffic fraction is configurable per-experiment among {2%, 5%, 10%, 20%, 50%, 100%} for LLM-42 runs.

verification.spec: Invoke the benchmark harness with --deterministic-fraction set to each of {0.02, 0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.50, 1.00}. For each run, confirm that the fraction of requests issued with is_deterministic=True approximately matches the configured value (within +/- 1% tolerance for large N), e.g., by inspecting request logs.

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced offline throughput for SGLang-Deterministic vs SGLang-Non-Deterministic on ShareGPT shows ~36% throughput penalty for deterministic mode.

verification.spec: Run offline throughput benchmark on ShareGPT dataset with 4096 requests using Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct on H100. Measure tokens/sec for SGLang-Non-Deterministic and SGLang-Deterministic modes. Compute fractional slowdown = 1 - (det_tput / non_det_tput). Verify slowdown ~0.36 (36%).

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced offline throughput shows SGLang-Deterministic penalty of ~24% on (in=2048, out=512) configuration.

verification.spec: Run offline throughput benchmark with synthetic workload of 4096 requests at input_length=2048, output_length=512 using Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct on H100. Measure tokens/sec for SGLang-Non-Deterministic and SGLang-Deterministic. Verify fractional slowdown ~0.24.

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced offline throughput for LLM-42 at 100% deterministic traffic outperforms SGLang-Deterministic in all configurations except ShareGPT (where it is only ~6% slower).

verification.spec: Run the offline throughput benchmark on all 8 configurations with LLM-42 at 100% deterministic and with SGLang-Deterministic. Compute the ratio LLM-42_tput / SGLang-Det_tput per config. Claim holds if ratio>=1.0 for 7/8 configs and ShareGPT ratio>=0.94 (i.e., at most ~6% slower).

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced offline throughput shows LLM-42 on ArXiv at 5%, 10%, 20%, and 50% deterministic traffic operates within 8%, 2%, 1%, and 1% respectively of SGLang-Non-Deterministic best case.

verification.spec: Run offline throughput benchmark on ArXiv dataset with 4096 requests using Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct on H100. Measure tokens/sec for SGLang-Non-Deterministic and LLM-42 at 5%, 10%, 20%, 50% deterministic traffic ratios. Compute fractional gap (1 - llm42_tput / non_det_tput). Verify gaps approximately 0.08, 0.02, 0.01, 0.01 respectively.

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced offline throughput shows LLM-42 is up to ~41% faster than SGLang-Deterministic on ArXiv at low deterministic ratios.

verification.spec: Run offline throughput on ArXiv for SGLang-Deterministic and LLM-42 @5%/10%/20% deterministic traffic. Compute speedup ratio LLM-42 / SGLang-Det at the best of these low-fraction settings. Expect ~1.41x (i.e., LLM-42 up to ~41% faster).

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced offline throughput shows LLM-42 is ~33% faster than SGLang-Deterministic on ShareGPT at 10% deterministic traffic.

verification.spec: Run offline throughput on ShareGPT for SGLang-Deterministic and LLM-42 @10% deterministic. Compute ratio LLM-42 / SGLang-Det; expect ~1.33x.

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced offline throughput shows LLM-42 is up to ~48% faster than SGLang-Deterministic on (in=2048, out=256) at 10% deterministic traffic.

verification.spec: Run offline throughput on synthetic workload in=2048/out=256 for SGLang-Deterministic and LLM-42 @10% deterministic with 4096 requests. Compute speedup; expect ~1.48x.

sub_tasks.requirements: Online inference experiments (Section 5.2) have been executed and reproduced.
Online inference benchmark on ShareGPT executes 4096 requests at QPS values of {12, 14, 16, 18}.

verification.spec: Launch the online benchmark on ShareGPT for QPS in {12, 14, 16, 18}, each sending 4096 requests via a Poisson arrival process. Confirm all 4 runs reach 4096 completed requests at each QPS, producing per-request latency logs.

sub_tasks.requirements: Online E2E latency CDFs are generated for SGLang-Non-Deterministic, SGLang-Deterministic, and LLM-42 at 2%, 5%, 10%, 20%, 50%, 100% deterministic traffic at each QPS.

verification.spec: For each QPS in {12,14,16,18}, run 8 system variants (SGLang-Non-Det, SGLang-Det, LLM-42 at 2/5/10/20/50/100% deterministic) on ShareGPT with 4096 requests. Confirm 32 latency CDF curves are produced and each contains 4096 latency points.

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced E2E latency at 12 QPS shows SGLang-Deterministic P50 ~4.64s and P99 ~28s, vs SGLang-Non-Deterministic P50 ~2.15s and P99 ~13.2s.

verification.spec: Run 4096 ShareGPT requests online @12 QPS with SGLang-Deterministic and SGLang-Non-Deterministic separately. Measure E2E latency percentiles. Verify SGLang-Deterministic P50 ~4.64s and P99 ~28s; SGLang-Non-Deterministic P50 ~2.15s and P99 ~13.2s.

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced E2E latency at 18 QPS shows SGLang-Deterministic P50 ~10.6s, P99 ~71.1s vs SGLang-Non-Deterministic P50 ~2.84s, P99 ~17.4s.

verification.spec: Run online inference on ShareGPT at 18 QPS with 4096 requests using Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct on H100. Measure P50 and P99 E2E latency for SGLang-Deterministic and SGLang-Non-Deterministic. Verify SGLang-Det P50 ~10.6s, P99 ~71.1s and SGLang-NonDet P50 ~2.84s, P99 ~17.4s.

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced E2E latency at 12 QPS shows LLM-42 @2% deterministic incurs only ~3% increase in median over SGLang-Non-Deterministic (~2.21s vs ~2.15s).

verification.spec: Run online inference benchmark on ShareGPT at 12 QPS with 4096 requests using Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct on H100. Run two configurations: (1) SGLang-Non-Deterministic baseline and (2) LLM-42 with 2% of requests marked is_deterministic=True. Measure P50 end-to-end request latency. Verify LLM-42@2% P50 is approximately 2.21s, within ~3% of non-deterministic baseline (~2.15s).

sub_tasks.requirements: Verification window characterization experiments (Figure 9) have been reproduced.
Per-token verification latency is profiled for verification window sizes {16, 32, 64, 128, 256, 512} on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct using an H100-PCIe GPU.

verification.spec: Profile the verification forward pass of LLM-42 (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, H100-PCIe) for window sizes {16,32,64,128,256,512}. Measure per-token latency = (forward_pass_ms / window_size). Confirm latencies form a decreasing curve, with peak ~0.75 ms at window 16 (or small) dropping to ~0.05 ms at window 512 (consistent with Figure 9a; ~15x reduction).

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced per-token verification cost shows ~0.75 ms at small window size and drops to ~0.05 ms at window size 512 (a ~15× reduction).

verification.spec: Measure per-token verification cost as (verifier_forward_pass_latency / window_size) for window sizes {16,32,64,128,256,512} on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct H100. Verify cost at window=512 is approximately 0.05 ms/token.

sub_tasks.requirements: Rollback characterization is performed by running 4096 ShareGPT requests at 12 QPS online for window sizes {32, 64, 128, 256}.

verification.spec: Run LLM-42 online on ShareGPT at 12 QPS with 4096 requests for each verification window size in {32,64,128,256} (deterministic for all requests). Collect per-request rollback counts and recomputed-token counts. Confirm a complete run for each window size and that aggregate stats are exported.

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced rollback distribution shows more than half of requests complete without any rollback for small window sizes.

verification.spec: Run LLM-42 on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct with ShareGPT at 12 QPS, window size 32, group size 8, processing 4096 requests. Record per-request rollback count and compute the fraction of requests with zero rollbacks. Expect >= 50% of requests to have zero rollbacks (per Figure 9b).

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced rollback distribution at window=256 shows ~40% of requests observe rollback ratio ≥ 50%, vs only ~5% at window=32.

verification.spec: Run 4096 ShareGPT requests through LLM-42 online @12 QPS with window=256, deterministic=100%. For each request compute rollback_ratio = (#verification_windows_with_mismatch / #verification_windows). Verify fraction of requests with rollback_ratio >= 0.5 is approximately 0.40.

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced total recomputation overhead approximately matches: ~6.81% at window=32, increasing roughly linearly to ~46.41% at window=256.

verification.spec: Run 4096 ShareGPT requests online @12 QPS, deterministic=100%, with window sizes {32, 256}. Compute recomputation overhead = (total_decoded_tokens - total_returned_tokens)/total_returned_tokens. Verify ~6.81% at window=32 and ~46.41% at window=256.

sub_tasks.requirements: Ablation study on grouped verification (Section 5.3, Figure 12) has been reproduced.

Ablation sweeps per-request window size ∈ {16, 32, 64, 128, 256, 512} and number of requests verified together ∈ {1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32}, with all 4096 ShareGPT requests requiring determinism, at 12 QPS online.

verification.spec: Run the grouped-verification ablation: sweep per-request window size in {16,32,64,128,256,512} and group size (requests verified together) in {1,2,4,8,16,32}, executing 4096 ShareGPT requests at 12 QPS with 100% deterministic traffic for each configuration. Confirm all 36 configurations complete and emit P99 latency and recompute cost metrics, matching the structure of Figure 12.

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced ablation shows that without grouped verification (batch size 1), P99 latency drops from ~615s at window=16 to ~56.18s at window=128, then rises to ~99.94s at window=512.

verification.spec: Run 4096 ShareGPT requests online @12 QPS with deterministic=100% and grouped-verification batch size = 1, sweeping window size in {16,128,512}. Measure P99 end-to-end latency. Verify window=128 yields ~56.18s P99 (the minimum), with window=16 at ~615s and window=512 at ~99.94s.
